behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the heuristic’s algorithmic quality, not infrastructure. A pure in-process test can compare heuristic route length vs. an exact/strong baseline solver on fixed coordinate sets; no OS/network/subprocess or AI output is required.
behaviors.test_description: Feed deterministic stop-coordinate fixtures (including edge layouts) into the heuristic function, compute optimal (or exact baseline for tractable subsets) in-process, and assert heuristic_length/optimal_length <= 1.5 for covered cases up to 20 stops.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic calculation logic (time = distance/speed with per-road-type config). Real failures are arithmetic/config-lookup defects that are catchable within a single object/function boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Call the estimator with explicit segment distances and road-type speed config, and assert per-segment and total window outputs (including rounding/units and mixed road types).

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is a pure validation rule on provided totals and remaining hours. No cross-module I/O boundary is required to expose rejection/excess-calculation bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke validator with total_estimated_time and remaining_shift_hours inputs around boundary conditions (equal, just over, far over) and assert rejection flag plus exact excess value.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: Real failure appears at module handoff: geocoding result handling -> stop filtering -> optimizer input -> skipped-address reporting. A unit test of only one function can miss wiring/state propagation defects. Higher-level System was considered but rejected because behavior text specifies logical API outcome ('returns no results'), which is reproducible with an in-process geocoder stub; no real HTTP transport behavior is required.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the service with a stub geocoding adapter that returns coordinates for N-1 addresses and empty result for one address; run full service call and assert (1) failed address is listed in skipped output, (2) optimizer receives only geocoded stops, (3) final route excludes skipped address.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is consolidation + downstream routing interaction: duplicates must merge correctly and route planner must not reintroduce duplicate visits. This is cross-module state handoff, not just isolated parsing logic. System/Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by behavior text: no real OS/network/subprocess requirement and no AI-dependent decision.
behaviors.test_description: Run service path with input containing duplicate address entries and distinct item payloads; assert output stop list has one merged stop with combined items and route visit sequence contains that address exactly once.
